gameMetadata.title: The Illusion of Success
gameMetadata.description: A 4-year university journey navigating the tension between societal expectations and authentic purpose.

nodes.text: Welcome to your freshman year. The next four years will dictate your future. Before the journey begins, you must declare your major. This choice will define the expectations society places upon you.
nodes.quote: "Man is ultimately self-determining." - Victor E. Frankl (133)

choices.text: Engineering - Chase the tech boom and build the infrastructure of tomorrow.
Humanities - Dive into the pursuit of history, narrative, and societal critique.

nodes.text: Your introductory engineering class is designed to weed people out. It feels like everyone around you is ahead of you. There is immense pressure to stand out.
nodes.quote: "That which does not kill me makes me stronger." - Victor E. Frankl (82)

choices.text: Cheat on the final project by copying an upperclassman's design.
Coast through your classes and decide to wait another year before applying to internships.
Build a small, open-source tool for a local charity at the expense of your time and grades.

nodes.text: You scored a prestigious interview, but the company builds addictive social media algorithms. Your peers are jealous.
nodes.quote: "The life that he has given to the object sets itself against him as an alien and horrible force." - Karl Marx (151)

choices.text: Ace the interview. Optimize the algorithm for maximum user retention.
Show up unprepared and bomb it intentionally to escape the pressure.
Use the interview to question their ethics. You get blacklisted.

nodes.text: Your open-source project was mocked by your peers for lacking ambition and not generating money. You're offered a chance to join one of the best hackathon teams on campus, but their project is a sketchy crypto exchange.
nodes.quote: "Groups are more immoral than individuals." - Martin Luther King Jr. (210).

choices.text: Join the team for the prize money and resume boost - the LinkedIn post after winning will feel great.
Skip the hackathon because you want to relax for the weekend.
Form a rival team to build a decentralized voting app. You lose the competition because your app doesn't have a "use case".

nodes.text: Coasting is catching up to you. The impostor syndrome is killing your self-esteem. A professor offers you a spot in a research lab, but it requires long and uncredited hours.
nodes.quote: "Sudden loss of hope and courage can have a deadly affect." - Viktor E. Frankl (75)

choices.text: Take the lab spot and steal a grad student's data to publish first.
Respectfully decline because you don't want any new responsibilities.
Take the lab spot, but insist on open-sourcing the data for public good.

nodes.text: You're quickly becoming one of the best engineers on campus. You're asked to build scrapes private user data for a club's marketing campaign. They tell you it's "standard practice".
nodes.quote: "An average person... could be perfectly incapable ot telling right from wrong." - Hannah Arendt (175)

choices.text: Write the program perfectly to receive a grant from the university.
Ask someone on fiverr to do it for you then take the credit.
Refuse and report the privacy violation, but lose your grant and reputation.

nodes.text: Every day feels the same. Today, you get grouped with highly motivated students for a major project. They tell you their idea is to build a chatbot that humanizes AI-generated essays.
nodes.quote: "A society in which consumption has to be artificially stimulated is a society founded on trash and waste." - John de Graaf

choices.text: Agree to start the venture, it could be profitable.
Let them do all the work while you just put your name on it.
Refuse and build your own project that helps students write better without cheating.

nodes.text: You are socially isolated but morally grounded. A local grassroots organization asks you to build a system to track corporate pollution, but it pays nothing and will take all your time.
nodes.quote: "For time is the essential ingredient; but in the modern world there is no time." - Carson (301)

choices.text: Abandon them, it's a waste of time anyway. Take a lucrative freelance gig to work on missile defense systems.
Tell them you are too busy and go back to sleep. Your homework is more important anyway.
Commit to the pollution tracker. Your grades tank, but the tool exposes a major leak.

nodes.text: You are obsessed with your starting salary. A defense contractor offers you a massive signing bonus to optimize drone targeting systems.
nodes.quote: "The virus is not confined to the upper class, but has found its way throughout our society." - De Graaf

choices.text: Sign the contract. You'll be set for life.
Ghost the recruiter - it's too tough of a decision.
Reject it, you take an offer from a local eco-friendly startup instead.

nodes.text: The engineering career fair is happening on campus, and you don't know if your resume is good enough to get you a job.
nodes.quote: "inequality and inequality central to life in the United States are greater drivers of this virus than the gross desire to consume." - Affluenza.

choices.text: Lie on your resume to get yourself a high-paying job.
Skip the career fair and hang out with some friends.
Go to the fair, but only hand resumes to local non-profits that pay minimum wage.

nodes.text: You're broke and your resume looks 'unconventional' to say the least. You discover a critical flaw in your university's cybersecurity system that exposes students' info.
nodes.quote: "I gradually gained a bit of satisfaction from being an extremist." - Martin Luther King Jr. (212)

choices.text: Blackmail the university for some money and a referral at a high-paying firm.
Just ignore it. Their issue has nothing to do with you.
Try to fix it, but you'll have to stay inside and drop everything else for a few days.

nodes.text: The burn out is real. You can't get enough caffeine or social validation. For your senior capstone, your team wants to build an application that could potentially automate millions of jobs.
nodes.quote: "The worker becomes a slave of the object." - Karl Marx (151)

choices.text: Take the lead on this one. The potential profitability is mouthwatering.
Let your team do all the work while you focus on job hunting.
Decide to build something that helps people get jobs instead.

nodes.text: Nothing is interesting anymore. You get assigned to maintain legacy code for the university library system. It's boring work that leaves you wanting more.
nodes.quote: "He would have a bad conscience only if he had not done what he had been ordered to do..." - Hannah Arendt (175)

choices.text: Change the codebase to charge students insanely high late fees to improve your own metrics.
Just make sure everything is working.
After getting approval from the library, make academic journals free for students.

nodes.text: No one seems to like you, but that doesn't stop you. You need to use a mssive amount of power for your capstone. The 'normal' way is to use GPUs that consume horrific amounts of energy and water.
nodes.quote: "Man can hardly recognize the devil of his own creation." - Carson (301)

choices.text: Take the easy route and use these resources. You need to graduate.
Make your project more basic to avoid the issue altogether.
Redesign the entire project to work on low-power embedded microcontrollers.

nodes.text: It's finals season and you have a massive job offer waiting. If your GPA drops it gets rescinded. You discover the professor's exam key is circulating on a private server...
nodes.quote: "He either wishes to do what other people do or he does what other people wish him to do." - Victor E. Frankl (106)

choices.text: Download the key. Everyone else is so you might as well. Besides, you need that job.
Ignore the key, but don't bother studying anyway.
Bring this to the attention to the professor. Everyone in your class is calling you a snitch.

nodes.text: Final exams hit and you're barely passing. A recruiter from a major defense firm reaches out, she says they're looking for new interns.
nodes.quote: "He had always acted against his "inclinations"..." - Hannah Arendt (181)

choices.text: Jump at this chance, it's the break you need..
You don't know how to respond, so you delete your draft.
Criticize their company practices and reach out to exciting startups instead.

nodes.text: You're respected by a few, but completely ignored by the corporate recruiters. A startup offers you a large amount of equity to develop an app that takes advantage of gig workers.
nodes.quote: "Alienated labor reverses the relationship... he... makes his life activity, his being, only a means for his existence." - Karl Marx (154)

choices.text: You're tired of being broke, miserable, and lonely. Accept the offer and get to building.
Decline the offer, but stop working on your own positive projects.
Decline the offer and create an open-source application for delivery drivers.

nodes.text: You're in the final stretch. You are 'successful.' You have the job, the money, the clout. But you feel completely empty. A massive tech conference invites you to speak on Innovation.
nodes.quote: "The more the worker declines in intelligence and becomes a slave of nature..." - Karl Marx (152)

choices.text: Deliver a standard speech generated by ChatGPT, sprinkle in some buzzwords along the way.
Cancel at the last minute due to stage fright.
Use the stage to publicly denounce the immoral practices of many firms in the industry. Preach change.

nodes.text: You're completely checked out. You have no job lined up and no motivation. Your friend offers you a referral at a company that makes homeownership difficult for the average person.
nodes.quote: "Lukewarm acceptance is more bewildering than outright rejection." - Martin Luther King Jr. (211)

choices.text: Take the referral because you need the money.
Say you'll apply, but never get around to it.
Refuse the referral and ask this friend to work on an app to help students with you.

nodes.text: You're barely scraping by financially, but you feel fine on the inside. You get nominated for a community service award, but attending means missing your final exam review.
nodes.quote: "Life's meaning is an unconditional one, for it even includes the potential meaning of unavoidable suffering." - Viktor E. Frankl (115)

choices.text: You need to go to this review session, so you skip the award completely.
Sleep through both the review and the award ceremony.
Attend the ceremony. You're unprepared for the exam, but you find the place you truly belong.

nodes.text: Graduation is tomorrow. You are signing the final paperwork for a life you aren't sure you want. A junior asks you for advice on how to succeed.
nodes.quote: "The iron cage will determine the lives of all individuals who are born into the mechanism... with irresistible force." - Max Weber (168)

choices.text: Tell them to put their head down, grind hard, and chase the bag no matter what.
Tell them it doesn't matter, it's all meaningless anyway.
Tell them to find their true self, to not let the expectations of society confine them to a narrow path.

nodes.text: Graduation is tomorrow. You coasted through four years and have nothing to show for it, but a piece of paper and a whole lot of regret.
nodes.quote: "Man... always decides what his existence will be, what he will become in the next moment." - Viktor E. Frankl (131)

choices.text: Panic and accept a soulless data-entry job just to have an income.
Accept your fate. Do nothing like you've been doing.
Decide it's not too late. Volunteer to teach STEM at a local middle school.

nodes.text: Graduation is tomorrow. You don't have a high-paying job lined up, but your tools are helping thousands. A corporate recruiter makes you a massive offer.
nodes.quote: "Even too much is not enough." - Affluenza

choices.text: You're tired of fighting, so you take the money and finally sell out.
Ignore the email and stare at the ceiling.
Delete the email, you don't need a cushy job to feel fulfilled.

nodes.text: Your introductory humanities seminar has so many interesting people and ideas, but your counselor is already steering you toward marketing and corporate strategy because there's more salary upside.
nodes.quote: "Freedom and responsibility are two sides of the same coin." - Victor E. Frankl (159)

choices.text: Take their advice and drop the seminar's optional reading group. Join the pre-business networking club instead. You need that money.
Stay in the seminar but stop paying attention. Skip the readings and just talk when you're asked.
Ignore the counselor. Dive headfirst into the seminar and start a new campus club where you can dive into these texts even deeper.

nodes.text: Your great writing catches the eye of a PR firm. They want you to lead a campaign burying negative press about a corporation involved in some shady business. They're paying you more money than you've ever seen in your life.
nodes.quote: "As he alienates his own activity, so he bestows upon the stranger activity that is not his own." - Karl Marx (156)

choices.text: Craft the campaign. You learn how to make immoral business dealings sound like generosity. That money helps at least.
Take the money but do sloppy, half-hearted work so the campaign fails.
Decline the contract and publish an article exposing the firm and this business to spread the truth.

nodes.text: Your blog gains a small following. A student media organization offers to absorb it with the condition that you accept corporate advertising and stop covering unheard voices on campus.
nodes.quote: "Injustice anywhere is a threat to justice everywhere." - Martin Luther King Jr. (209)

choices.text: Accept the deal, the blog can reach more people, so you'll get more publicity.
Slowly stop publishing, it's too time consuming.
Keep the blog independent. Your readership stays small, but at least you're telling real stories.

nodes.text: You're withdrawing further. A screenwriting professor spots your raw talent and offers to connect you with an entertainment agent that manages content for an unethical streaming platform.
nodes.quote: "It is wrong to use immoral means to attain moral ends." - Martin Luther King Jr. (214)

choices.text: Pursue the agent and get the gig. That money will help a lot.
Don't respond to the professor, you don't know if that's something you'd want to do.
Decline and focus your efforts on helping real businesses benefit others.

nodes.text: You're developing a reputation as a skilled writer and researcher. A political consulting firm offers you a high paying internship to write misinformation about gerrymandering.
nodes.quote: "He had never harbored any ill feelings about his victims." - Hannah Arendt (177)

choices.text: Write the content. It's technically legal, so you think that's fine.
Take the deposit and write something vague and unusable. Keep the money because why not.
Refuse and write a public thread documenting the firm's request in detail.

nodes.text: You've been drifting between classes and deadlines. A campus nonprofit asks for help writing grant applications, but showing up consistently feels impossible.
nodes.quote: "The more one forgets himself... the more human he is and the more he actualizes himself." - Viktor E. Frankl

choices.text: Force yourself to commit, then turn the applications into polished portfolio pieces for recruiting season.
Say yes, then miss meetings and go quiet when they follow up.
Show up consistently and help secure funding so the nonprofit can keep its tutoring program open.

nodes.text: Your independent journalism is reaching people. A prominent investigative news outlet wants your story on campus labor organizing but they want you to reveal your anonymous sources.
nodes.quote: "It is wrong to use immoral means to attain moral ends." - Martin Luther King Jr. (214)

choices.text: Name the source so that the story can run. The source gets expelled, but you get the rep.
Initially agree, but decide you can't go through with it.
Protect the source by publishing a shorter, source-free version independently.

nodes.text: You have clout in media circles. A major news network offers a prestigious internship where you will frame poverty as a personal failing.
nodes.quote: "Society must protect the robbed and punish the robber." - Martin Luther King Jr. (211)

choices.text: Accept. You absorb the framing until it feels like your own voice.
Accept the internship but produce nothing, you're stuck between compliance an rejection
Decline and instead place yourself within a local community newspaper covering housing displacement.

nodes.text: You're forced to attend a mandatory career seminar for humanities majors. The keynote speaker is an alum who writes crisis PR for an oil company who says they're offering 'easy, high-paying summer internships'.
nodes.quote: "He lost the need to feel anything at all." - Hannah Arendt (180)

choices.text: Your resume is empty, but you still apply for the PR internship and write exactly what they want to hear.
Leave the seminar early to back to your dorm and sleep.
Reject the corporate lifeline. Spend the night furiously applying to an unpaid fellowship at a civil rights archive instead.

nodes.text: Your independent work has a loyal following. You uncover evidence that the university is ghost-writing positive editorials about itself and submitting them to the student newspaper under fake names.
nodes.quote: "One day the South will recognize its real heroes." - Martin Luther King Jr. (214)

choices.text: You can't lose your scholarship so you do keep the evidence to yourself.
Give the evidence to your friend and let them decide how to deal with it.
Go public with it. You risk your scholarship, but the university ends up offering an apology.

nodes.text: You're polished and employable. For your capstone project, your advisor pushes you toward a 'safe' academic analysis.
nodes.quote: "The human being is completely and unavoidably influenced by his surroundings." - Victor E. Frankl (65)

choices.text: Write the safe project to guarantee that A, but you feel like you weren't true to yourself.
Start your personal project, then abandon it halfway. Turn in something unfinished to get the job done.
Write the true story of your family's displacement. Your professor gives you a C, but it's the most important thing you have ever written.

nodes.text: You spend most days skipping class and scrolling insta reels. To stay enrolled, you must declare a capstone project. A classmate lets you add your name to their work, giving you credit for doing nothing.
nodes.quote: "A man's concern, even in his despair over the unworthiness of his life is an existential disease..." - Viktor E. Frankl

choices.text: You take the credit and leverage the project's metrics to secure a lucrative interview with a corporate ad-tech firm.
Accept the free credit. Put your name on the title slide, skip the actual presentation, and go back to sleep.
You refuse the easy out and scramble to propose a rigorous study on the psychological decay caused by ad-tech.

nodes.text: Your housing displacement coverage gets traction. A real estate developer threatens legal action to silence your reporting. A media law clinic can defend you for free, but the case will consume so much time.
nodes.quote: "The most flagrant abuses go unchecked in both state and federal agencies." - Carson (304)

choices.text: Retract the story to avoid the case altogether.
Go silent and focus on schoolwork.
Fight the legal threat with the clinic. The case takes a year, but you win.

nodes.text: A firm offers an internship writing SEO articles designed to spread health misinformation under the guise of wellness content, but the stipend is great
nodes.quote: "Have we fallen into a mesmerized state that makes us accept as inevitable that which is inferior or detrimental..." - Carson (304)

choices.text: Take the offer. You write a hundred articles undermining public health. You buy a nice apartment.
Take the job but write articles so bad they never publish them. Collect paychecks and feel hollow.
Decline. Spend the semester writing a reader's guide to identifying health misinformation and post it on your free blog.

nodes.text: The pressure to secure a summer internship is suffocating. You've been dodging the career center, but a recruiter for a notorious firm for misinformation offers you a 'Sustainability PR' internship.
nodes.quote: "Capitalism is a ... mere game, a competitive sport." - Max Weber (167).

choices.text: The anxiety of being unemployed is too much, so yo take the internship convince yourself that you had to.
Politely take their business card, throw it in the trash the moment they look away, and hop on the PS5 back at your dorm
You publicly reject the offer and spend the weekend organizing a boycott of the brand, getting blacklisted by the career center.

nodes.text: Your legal fight made you known among nonprofits. A small nonprofit journal offers you a staff position. The pay is next to none, but you have full ownership of your work.
nodes.quote: "A life whose meaning depends on such a happenstance... ultimately would not be worth living at all." - Viktor E. Frankl (115)

choices.text: Turn it down. You need a livable wage. Take a corporate content strategy role instead.
Accept the job offer, then spend the first month staring at a blank document, overwhelmed.
Accept and throw yourself into the work. Your first piece changes a local election.

nodes.text: Your resume is polished and you've been well-compensated. A junior classmate has written a devastating, important piece about someone plagiarizing their work. They ask you to write and publish something about it.
nodes.quote: "Injustice anywhere is a threat to justice everywhere." - Martin Luther King Jr. (209)

choices.text: Decline the request because you need a referral from someone named in the piece.
Offer to help, but stall for a while.
Use every connection you have to get it published. You lose that referral, but gain something more.

nodes.text: The reality of graduation hits like a freight train. Outside your window, the quad is swarming with peers in sharp suits heading to the mega-corporate career fair. You have no job lined up, no capstone topic, and no plan. The academic safety net you've been relying on is no more.
nodes.quote: "Life ultimately means taking the responsibility to find the right answer to its problems and fulfill the tasks which it constantly sets out for each individual." - Viktor E. Frankl (77)

choices.text: Throw on a borrowed suit, sprint to the fair, and aggressively pitch yourself for a soulless B2B sales role at a firm just to feel secure.
Close the blinds. Turn up your music to drown out the anxiety, crawl back into bed, and pretend the future doesn't exist.
Walk out to the quad, but not to interview. You spend the entire day handing out flyers exposing the environmental and labor violations of the top firms recruiting there.

nodes.text: Your work is honest and your integrity is intact. A large national publication offers you a staff job, but you'll have to sign away the rights to all your previous independent work, including your family displacement story.
nodes.quote: "Meaning... gives him capability to cope with suffering." - Viktor E. Frankl

choices.text: You need the money, so you sell the rights. All of your work is no longer yours.
Let the offer sit in your inbox for a few weeks.
Walk away from the offer. Publish your family story on the internet.

nodes.text: You're a skilled writer with a comfortable career ahead of you. A first-year student shows you their raw, urgent, true piece of writing and asks if they should pursue this path.
nodes.quote: "Iron Cage" - Max Weber

choices.text: Tell them to learn SEO, build a brand, and monetize. There's no point in doing something that won't help your career.
Tell them you don't know. Shrug. Walk to your graduation.
Tell them their story is the most important thing in the world and someone needs to tell it. Staying true to yourself is key.

nodes.text: Graduation is tomorrow. The campus is buzzing with final celebrations and panicked goodbyes. You sit in your half-packed room, staring at a blank wall.
nodes.quote: "Boredom... causes more problems to solve than distress." - Viktor E. Frankl

choices.text: Put on your cap and gown. Walk across the stage and step into the void.

nodes.text: Semester 8. Graduation. You have no staff job lined up, but your work has impacted the lives of many and you feel fulfilled. A media company gives you one final offer.
nodes.quote: "Society must protect the robbed and punish the robber." - Martin Luther King Jr. (211)

choices.text: Accept. You tell yourself that you'll try to change the company culture, but you'll slowly fall into their ways.
Leave the email unread. Procrastinate until the offer expires.
Decline. Start an independent media cooperative with three other graduating students who feel the same way about making a real impact in the world.

epilogueLogic.description: When the game reaches 'epilogue_trigger', the UI drops the visible stats entirely. The game reads the final hidden stats to deliver the conclusion.

conditions.title: Trapped in the Iron Cage
conditions.text: 10 Years Later: You have everything society told you to want. You are incredibly wealthy and highly respected. But your work has systematically harmed others, automated away jobs, and extracted resources without giving back. You stare at your massive bank account, completely alienated from your own life. The dangerous illusion worked.
conditions.quote: The cloak should become an iron cage. - Max Weber

conditions.title: You Found Your Purpose
conditions.text: 10 Years Later: Your resume is a mess. You never made the 30-under-30 list. But the systems you built empower people. You rejected the status quo, and while society punished you with lower pay and less prestige, you sleep soundly. You broke the illusion, and in doing so, you found out what actually matters.
conditions.quote: He who has a why to live, can bear almost any how. - Viktor E. Frankl & Friedrich Nietzsche

conditions.title: You Wasted The Most Important Years Of Your Life
conditions.text: 10 Years Later: You avoided doing evil, but you also avoided doing good. You coasted through the system, neither conforming deeply nor rebelling meaningfully. You escaped the worst of the corporate machine, but you failed to find your authentic purpose.
conditions.quote: "Lukewarm acceptance is more bewildering than outright rejection." - Martin Luther King Jr. (211)

conditions.title: The Choice Is Still Yours
conditions.text: 10 Years Later: You compromised. You took some of the money, and you tried to do some good on the side. You are comfortable, but the tension between the societal illusion and your inner authenticity remains a daily battle.
conditions.quote: Live as if you were living the second life and had acted as wrongly the first time as you are about to act now. - Viktor E. Frankl
